frames.description: the ground truth — what is actually happening behind the early bell
what Aldous believes (diverges from the truth)
what Maren believes about her cousin's death

branches.description: Aldous carries word to the mainland village
Aldous confronts Father Crispin directly in the tower
Aldous seizes the bell and rings on his own judgment

entities.description: Father Crispin, prior and bell-keeper of thirty years
a mainland fisherwoman, the community's link
Maren's cousin, master of the Kittiwake, a smuggler
the mainland harbourmaster who keeps the landing ledger
the great bronze tower bell that guides the boats home
the worn tide-stone Crispin reads the channel depth by
Crispin's brass reading-glass, used to sight the far channel marks

smuggled cargo run by the Kittiwake
the shifting marsh channel that floods twice a day

predicates.description: Crispin's ability to read the dusk channel by sight and sound
what the early bell actually is
the real cause of the Kittiwake's loss
who holds the bell-rope on a given night

facts.claim: Aldous arrives as Crispin's new apprentice bellwright; the dusk bell is rung to guide the fishing boats through the shifting channel before the light fails.
Crispin reads the dusk crossing by two tools: the worn tide-stone he feels for depth and the brass reading-glass he sights the far channel marks with.
In truth Crispin's eyes and ears have been failing for two years; without the tide-stone and glass he can no longer be sure of the dusk channel.
At an ordinary dusk Aldous watches Crispin sight the marks with the brass glass and ring the bell on time; every boat comes home safe.
Maren brings fish to the island and speaks of her cousin Edrick, master of the Kittiwake, a boat that 'fishes where she shouldn't' and stays out past the light.
On a fog-thick night Crispin rings the bell a full hour early, while the channel is still certainly passable, and will not say why.

The early bell is an act of caution and penance: on nights his failing senses cannot trust the true dusk, Crispin rings early to bring every boat in while the channel is sure, accepting a short fishing day over a fatal misread.
Aldous opens the tide-ledger and finds the early-bell nights marked, and beside one of them the line 'Kittiwake — did not come.'
In truth the Kittiwake was lost not to the bell but to Edrick's own greed: he stayed out past the early bell to run contraband and misjudged the channel in the dark.
Crispin knows the Kittiwake died smuggling and has kept silent to spare Edrick's family the shame.
Aldous comes to believe the early bell is no mercy but a snare — that Crispin rings early to wreck boats on the marsh for their salvage.
Maren believes the early bell is what killed her cousin Edrick, calling him in wrong and drowning the Kittiwake in the dark.
At the marks Aldous sees Crispin squint, lean to hear the bar-surf, and grip the brass glass with a shaking hand — the true sign that the old keeper's senses have failed.
Aldous reads Crispin's straining at the marks not as failing senses but as a guilty man pretending, hiding what he does with the early bell.
A storm-fog is rising; tomorrow's dusk crossing will be the most dangerous of the season, and Aldous must choose his road — expose Crispin, confront him, or take the bell himself.
Aldous rows to the mainland and tells Maren and the village elders that the early bell is murder and Crispin a wrecker.
The village, already grieving the Kittiwake, is roused against Crispin and means to bring him to account.
The harbourmaster lays out his landing ledger: on every early-bell night every boat but one came home, and the early ring matched a night the dusk channel had already shut.
The harbourmaster reveals the contraband recovered from the wreck: the Kittiwake was lost smuggling past the bell, by Edrick's own choice.
Faced with the ledger and the contraband, Aldous's belief that the bell was a snare collapses, and he sees the early ring for the mercy it was.
Maren learns her cousin died by his own greed, not by the bell, and grieves the harder for it.
Crispin is exposed and vindicated at once: cleared of the charge, but too shamed by his failed senses to keep the bell.
Crispin steps down and names Aldous bellwright in his place; the truth of the early bell is now the village's own, and the marsh keeps its dead.
That night Aldous climbs the tower and accuses Crispin to his face of ringing the bell to wreck boats.
Crispin does not deny the early bell, only the reason, and the old keeper's silence finally breaks.

Crispin confesses the failing of his eyes and ears, and the old guilt behind it: years ago he rang late, misread a true dusk, and lost a boat — the early bell is his penance and his caution ever since.
Crispin sets the worn tide-stone and the brass glass in Aldous's hands and shows that he can no longer feel the depth nor sight the marks.
Hearing the confession and holding the stone, Aldous understands the early bell was mercy, not murder, and lays his suspicion down.
The storm-fog night comes; Crispin, unable to read the worst dusk of the season, hands the bell-rope to Aldous.

Using the tide-stone and glass Crispin gave him, Aldous reads the channel and rings true and on time; every boat comes home, and the secret of Edrick's smuggling is kept between them for Maren's sake.
Certain the early bell is a killer, on the storm-fog night Aldous locks Crispin from the tower and takes the bell to ring it 'rightly' — at the true dusk, never early.
From the locked stair Crispin calls that the boy cannot read the dusk without the stone and the glass, but Aldous will not listen.
Aldous waits at the marks for what he takes to be the true dusk, sure he is doing what Crispin would not.

Without the tide-stone to feel the depth or the glass to sight the marks, Aldous misreads the storm-fog exactly as Crispin once did: the channel has already shut, and the dusk he waits for is the wrong one.
Aldous rings at the late dusk he chose; a boat reads the wrong bell and is lost in the shifting marsh, the very death the early bell had always prevented.
Aldous understands too late that the early bell was mercy, not murder, and that the killer he hunted was his own certainty; Crispin, broken, takes the rope back and rings on.
